questionText: A particle moves so that its position at time $t$ is $x = e^{t}$, $y = e^{-t}$. (a) Show that the path is part of the curve $xy = 1$. (b) Find the exact speed of the particle when $t = \ln 2$.

steps.explanation: Multiplying $x$ and $y$ makes the exponents add to zero, so $xy=1$ for every value of $t$; the path lies on this rectangular hyperbola.

steps.description: Differentiate x with respect to t
steps.explanation: The exponential $e^{t}$ differentiates to itself, giving the horizontal velocity.

steps.description: Differentiate y with respect to t
steps.explanation: By the chain rule $e^{-t}$ differentiates to $-e^{-t}$, giving the vertical velocity.
Speed is the magnitude of the velocity, found by combining the two components with Pythagoras.

steps.description: Evaluate the exponentials at t = ln 2
steps.explanation: Because exponential and logarithm are inverse operations, these simplify to exact fractions.
Substituting the values above gives the two components at this instant.
Squaring removes the sign and prepares the two terms for adding.
Writing $4$ as $\tfrac{16}{4}$ lets us combine the terms into a single fraction under the root.
The square root of the denominator is $2$, giving the exact speed in tidy surd form.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: (a) Since $xy = e^{t}e^{-t} = 1$, the path lies on the hyperbola $xy=1$. (b) The exact speed at $t=\ln 2$ is $\dfrac{\sqrt{17}}{2}$ units per unit time.
